nativeRange.habitat: Shallow seagrass beds (particularly Zostera species), coral reefs, and mangroves; depth range 0–10 m. Strongly associated with seagrass microhabitats in calm, sheltered coastal waters.

diet.notes: Requires a near-continuous supply of live food due to a short digestive tract and extremely fast metabolism. Newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii (Artemia) are the staple diet; copepods, rotifers, and small shrimp larvae are accepted and beneficial. Has been documented consuming up to 3,000 brine shrimp nauplii per day. Adults may accept frozen cyclopods or enriched nauplii but generally do best on live prey. Fry require live copepods (e.g. Tisbe biminiensis) from birth.

goodWith: small pipefish (with caution — may outcompete for food)
avoidWith: live rock or live sand with stinging animals

summary: Tiny, sociable seahorse from Gulf of Mexico seagrass beds, reaching around 2.5–5 cm. Generally considered advanced care due to its need for live food nearly continuously and sensitivity to water quality.
careNotes: Hippocampus zosterae is the smallest seahorse species sold in the hobby and one of the most demanding to keep. A dedicated species tank is strongly advised — even small pipefish may outcompete them for live food. A sponge filter (not a power filter) is generally recommended, as these slow-moving fish can be sucked into intakes. Tank size tends to be unusually small for a marine fish: 5 gallons (19 L) is commonly cited as the minimum for a pair, with 10 gallons (38 L) recommended for groups of four or more. Paradoxically, very large tanks can make it hard for them to find enough food. Groups of 4+ individuals tend to breed more readily and have been reported to thrive better than isolated pairs. Hitching posts — branching gorgonians, Caulerpa macroalgae, artificial coral, or sea whips — are important for their well-being. Water flow should be minimal; strong current stresses these fish. Temperature: generally advised to keep around 22–26°C (72–79°F); FishBase records wild individuals at up to 27.8°C, but aquarium sources typically recommend keeping below 26°C. Salinity: averaged across sources at SG 1.020–1.024 (sources ranged from 1.019–1.025). The species is not considered reef-safe per LiveAquaria (risk from stinging invertebrates in live rock). IUCN status: Data Deficient. Source disagreement on reef safety: LiveAquaria lists 'No'; this is primarily a precaution against stinging animals on live rock, not because the species actively harms corals. Source disagreement on temperature max: FishBase environmental envelope extends to 27.8°C; aquarium sources favor 24–26°C; averaged max of 26°C used. Lifespan: wild individuals typically survive about 1 year; captive specimens have been reported to live 2+ years with attentive care.
breedingNotes: Among the most prolific breeders in the seahorse family. Males carry eggs in a ventral brood pouch for approximately 10–14 days before releasing 2–55 fry (commonly 10–30) that are fully independent at birth, measuring about 7–9 mm. Reproductive cycles repeat every 10–14 days under favorable conditions; males may produce up to two broods per month. Has been reported to breed readily in captivity provided 12+ hours of lighting and stable water quality. Fry require live copepod nauplii (e.g. Tisbe biminiensis) or rotifers from birth; newly hatched Artemia nauplii are generally too large for newborn fry. Groups of 10 or more individuals tend to show higher breeding success than smaller groups.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species-specific product page for Hippocampus zosterae. Primary source for care level (Difficult), tank size (5 gal minimum), water parameters, diet, temperament, and reef-safe status. Note: the manifest supplied the seahorses collection URL; actual species page used.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 5.2 cm SL), temperature range (23.4–27.8°C), native range (Western Atlantic: Bermuda, Florida, Bahamas, Gulf of Mexico), taxonomy (Syngnathidae / Syngnathiformes), and maximum reported age (1 year in wild). Temperature max disagreement: FishBase 27.8°C vs aquarium sources ~24–26°C; averaged max 26°C used.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Dwarf Seahorse)
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 5.0 cm), lifespan (up to 2 years captivity), native range (Florida, Bahamas, Gulf of Mexico, Texas), habitat (seagrass beds), temperature (20–28°C), and diet (small crustaceans, amphipods, shrimp). Used for native range and lifespan verification.

additional.name: The Aquarium Adviser — Dwarf Seahorse Care
additional.notes: Third-party care guide used for salinity cross-check (1.022–1.024 SG), temperature (22–24°C), lifespan (~2 years captivity), and tank size confirmation (5 gal minimum). Salinity averaged across LiveAquaria (1.020–1.025), this source (1.022–1.024), and Ocean Rider (1.019–1.022) to yield 1.020–1.024.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeds readily in captivity; male broods 2–55 fry in ventral pouch for 10–14 days. Fry are independent at birth (~7–9 mm) but require live nauplii. Cycle can repeat up to twice per month.
